justification: All 5 expected rules are identified and exceeded (9 total). State machine directionality is explicitly modeled (BR-3: in_transit→pending forbidden, BR-4: delivered is terminal). 15 acceptance criteria all use Given/When/Then format. Testable assertions are concrete and developer-actionable. Weight bracket rules (BR-7/8/9) correctly marked invariant: false. Confidence at 82 with clearly stated assumptions. Minor nuance: BR-6 (max 1000 kg cap) marked invariant: false with reasonable justification ('business policy limit, not physical law'), whereas rubric bundles it with the weight constraint as invariant: true — but the reasoning is defensible and well-argued.

observations.text: Exceeded rule count (9 vs 5 expected) with thoughtful granular decomposition — split weight validation into positive-check and cap-check, and enumerated all three bracket boundaries separately
State machine modeled with full directionality: in_transit→pending forbidden (BR-3), delivered terminal (BR-4), and even caught the Pending→Delivered skip path (AC-15) as an edge case
All 15 acceptance criteria use strict Given/When/Then format with concrete preconditions and specific expected outcomes
Testable assertions are highly specific: 'Any attempt to create a shipment missing either pickup or delivery address must be rejected' — directly implementable as a test
Weight bracket boundaries correctly use inclusive/exclusive semantics (e.g., [10, 50) for Heavy) with exact boundary edge cases tested (AC-11: exactly 10 kg → Heavy, AC-13: exactly 50 kg → Freight)
Confidence at 82 with three explicit assumptions and clear separation of high/low confidence areas — low confidence area (Pending→Delivered skip) is a genuine inference gap
Edge cases section identifies 8 boundary scenarios including 0.001 kg and 1000.01 kg — shows systematic boundary-value thinking
Business impact analysis ties rules to stakeholder consequences (revenue leakage from bracket errors, audit trail corruption from state regression)
BR-6 (1000 kg cap) classified as invariant: false — rubric expects weight constraints as invariant: true, though the agent's reasoning ('business policy, not physical law') is defensible
